有史以来功能最强大、功能最多的Web浏览器正在变成一个“应用”，采用它的理由越来越少，因为今天的Firefox不再以用户为中心，而更多地以Mozilla为中心(有流行语、政治迎合和营销，而不是技术实质)。
摘要：Mozilla的商业模式不断恶化，因为“app”思维和/或“社交媒体”思维被实际(长期)用户的需求所取代，以“性能”或“简单”的名义限制了Firefox浏览器的可扩展性(好像用户所需要的只是“黑暗模式”和搜索引擎的选择)。
多年来，我们写了几十篇关于Mozilla和Firefox的文章，最初大多是祝贺的(那时发布很稳定，很少见)，但最近我们变得更具批判性，因为Mozilla不再是原来的公司了。当然，它比微软好得多，但随着时间的推移，它正在变得更糟，而不是更好。早在裁员之前，我们就已经警告说，已经采取的方向是错误的。它疏远了开发者和用户--这正是Mozilla十多年来赖以生存的东西。甚至在DRM和“Eich-Gate”等崩溃之前，就存在与隐私相关的问题，这在Mozilla[1，2][1，2](或一种形式的营销)中现在只是一种空洞的言辞。
Mozilla不是GNU项目；事实上，有Firefox forks是GNU项目。我们认为Mozilla不会像GNU那样捍卫自由，但这不是重点。Mozilla似乎不仅放弃了自由，也放弃了开发者和用户。这是一条自杀之路。如果没有来自公司外部的动力，Mozilla可能在财务上是不可行的；它长期以来一直依赖于一支志愿者大军，既有开发人员，也有“营销人员”(或倡导者)。失去它们不是一种选择，但Mozilla似乎忽略了真正让Firefox如此受欢迎的最初原因。
丹尼尔(‘坎塔’)最近写了一篇关于这个主题的不错的文章，由我和他从西班牙语翻译/策划。
今天早些时候，我更新了Mozilla Firefox，我几乎不再使用它(我在较老的机器上混合使用Konallor、Falkon和QupZilla)。事实上，当我发现这一更新或“升级”(更新得多的版本)在几个方面让事情变得更糟时，我真的有点吓坏了。
有些事情可以通过改变设置来“修复”，但有些事情是不可修复的。“虽然一些DRM控制的内容可以使用Adobe Flash插件观看，但许多服务正在转向HTML5视频，它需要一种不同的DRM机制，称为内容解密模块(CDM)，”Firefox引导我进入的页面说。拥抱DRM并没有帮助或拯救Mozilla，不是吗？这可能只是疏远了许多像我这样的人，他们过去常常向人们倡导和推荐Firefox。
当今天早上“升级”完成时，几个可用性问题变得明显起来。但还有更糟糕的事情。当它‘升级’到最新的esr时，Mozilla(可能不是Debian Packages)在默认情况下打开了“由袖珍推荐”，实际上每当我打开选项卡…时，它都会向我吐出废话(精神噪音/杂乱)
现在的Mozilla当然感觉像是把Firefox当作社交媒体思维的延伸。相反，它应该与之作斗争/反击。但是看看现在是谁在运营mozilla，…。微软和Facebook的高管。
曾几何时，Mozilla吸引了极客，他们随后向朋友、同事，特别是家人推荐了Firefox(比如孩子和父母，他们不一定对电脑充满热情，只是点击了“互联网”，这就是他们所说的蓝色“E”)。如今，Mozilla鼓励开发人员有计划地淘汰(我在扩展/主题意义上为Firefox做了一些贡献)，或者是一度(或十多年来)运行良好的插件/扩展的“数字腐烂”。与2020年相比，Firefox在2005年有了更多有用的扩展(我应该知道，我在2004年就接受了Firefox；在此之前，我曾使用过Netscape和Mozilla)。例如，XUL不应该被抛弃，但他们又一次比用户和志愿者开发者更关心钱(向CEO支付超过200万美元的年薪，外加奖金)。
Mozilla对当前管理团队(百万富翁)的底线来说可能工作得不错，但对于我们中的许多人来说就不管用了，他们需要一个“大浏览器”来挑战日益专有的网络浏览垄断/寡头垄断。除了Firefox，所有的“大浏览器”(被广泛支持并被认为是“必须支持的”)都是专有的，通常都有一些公开的倾向。
如果Mozilla不再支持自由开放的网络，如果Waterfox成为监控行业的延伸(与StartPage的所有者相同)，那么整个壁虎大家庭都将成为一个失败的事业或失败的战略。围绕GAFAM的单一文化将使Web成为DRM的专利，有必要转向替代方案(Web本身，而不仅仅是Web浏览器，因为DRM现在已经并入Web标准)。
最后一点：虽然我同意Mozilla在大多数问题上的政治取向，但我希望不要每次我打开新的标签页时都把Mozilla的话硬塞进我的喉咙(或脑袋)。这种“用户体验”(用户体验)是非自由软件的标志，也是Mozilla正在快速发展的症状。Web浏览器的用户应该完全控制浏览器，在推送某人的社论消息时，不必依赖任何第三方来评估、排名或审查页面。浏览器应该呈现页面，而不是某人的广告牌。尊重人们的判断，抵制成为“网络保姆”的诱惑(即使是在应用层/层)。█。
如果你喜欢这篇文章，可以考虑订阅RSS提要，或者现在就加入我们的IRC频道。
[迷因]GNU/Linux版的Openwashed(和间谍)杀手应用程序？一个基本的Windows计算器！Linux上没有比另一个移植到GNU/Linux上的无用计算器(这个计算器带有臭名昭著的微软遥测)更重要、更紧迫的新闻了。
开源调查盖茨的录像带及其与时事的关系媒体告诉我们的所谓的慈善家和捐赠者比尔·盖茨突然变得(自从大流行开始他又赚了1000万美元)需要进一步曝光，因为在突袭了软件界(通过违法垄断)之后，他现在在其他领域也做了同样的事情。
被认为温和的比尔·盖茨被问及所谓的“圣战”我们重温了奇怪的用词(盖茨先生在内部反复使用)，暗示着商业环境中某种神圣的战争/武器呼唤。
为什么微软不接受GNU/Linux(取代Windows)比尔·盖茨的证词表明，盖茨意识到Windows已经成为市场垄断，并试图利用这一点来推动他认为可以保护微软垄断地位的任何“杀手级应用”；这些策略从未改变(这里有两天前的一个例子：“微软正在将Office Web应用强加给边缘用户”)。
Mozilla的悲惨故事越来越悲哀，因为Mozilla的管理者抛弃了用户，选择了公司作为他们的客户。Mozilla的商业模式不断恶化，因为App的心态和/或社交控制媒体的心态被选作了实际(长期)用户的需求，以性能或简单性的名义限制了Firefox浏览器的可扩展性(就好像用户需要的只是黑暗模式一样)，而不是实际(长期)用户的需求，从而限制了Firefox浏览器的可扩展性(就像用户所需要的只是黑暗模式一样)，从而使火狐浏览器的可扩展性变得更加悲哀，因为Mozilla的经理们抛弃了用户，选择了公司作为他们的客户，Mozilla的商业模式不断恶化，因为应用程序的心态和/或社交媒体的心态被放在了实际(长期)用户的需求之上，从而限制了Firefox浏览器的可扩展性(就像用户需要的只是黑暗模式一样)。和搜索引擎的选择)。
你可能需要停止战争来阻止微软今天，公司继续游说，要求更极端的版权和更少的公开表达权。政府偏袒游说者，通过莱西格承认(在达特茅斯大学的一次演讲中)基本上是合法贿赂的制度。
科技很难，但变得不可知和漠不关心就更难了那些对科技在过去几十年里采取的敌对方向有了更多了解的人明智地避免了其中的大部分；在一个前所未有的企业政变时期，传达危险对社会的诚信非常重要(通过追踪联系人和其他以健康和团结的名义开出的替代医学)。
[迷因]当Linux新闻成为点击诱饵，甚至更糟的时候，我们再次看到，在微软采取行动之前，媒体忽视了一些事情，而在这种情况下，它被认为是值得推广的。在这种情况下，我们又一次看到，在微软采取行动之前，媒体会忽视这些事情，在这种情况下，它被认为是值得推广的。
FIDO/FIDO2对溢价的虚假安全感来自对软件自由的攻击(包括Richard Stallman和其他领导人/名人)我们已经看到攻击隐私本身的转变，例如可审计的加密；今天我们讨论FIDO/FIDO2领域令人不安的发展。
ClearlyDefined只是微软的Land Grab(OSI现在积极参与其中)今天的OSI不是自由软件定义甚至开放源码定义的保护者；它正变得越来越小，只是服务于微软议程的一个容器，集中了微软可以指挥和控制的东西
Linus Torvalds谈GNU时，他的自我还很小，二十多年前，Linux的创建者解释了他的项目与GNU的关系/连接(这比他的项目早了几乎整整十年，也给了他灵感)；现在他假装GNU几乎不存在，Linux基金会也假装GNU只是Linux的一部分。
GNU/Linux采用和自由软件增长的第二波证据表明，在专有软件巨头灭亡的同时，越来越多的人转向自由软件(本月早些时候也有一项关于这一点的最新调查)；大流行所起的作用是可以衡量的，专有软件巨头将随着它的继续而苦苦挣扎。
激进主义和自由软件争取软件自由的斗争应该继续下去；软件在从我们这里被偷走之前是免费的，很快就变成了事实上的私人垄断，这些大亨聚集了软件专利，并购买了对所有工作的控制权(例如GitHub)。
GNU和Linux新闻网站在2020年(大流行年)的健康报告以GNU/Linux为重点的网站恶化(新闻、教学材料、分析等)。这是一个日益严重的问题；它似乎很多都变成了垄断企业的私有财产，许多项目现在由微软(通过GitHub)间接控制，而有凝聚力的社区声音却被系统性地压制了，公民新闻似乎失去了动力。
比尔·盖茨谎言的抄本：第四部分(最后)Techright正在策划和维护1998年盖茨证词的纯文本版本，在这份证词中，公司数年(如果不是几十年的话)的滥用行为受到了面对面的审查。
对“微软关闭黑客行动”的回应我回复了选举-Vote.com，因为他们提到微软是好人，DaemonFC写道。取下一些勒索软件(当然是和律师一起)。
我们已经将UPC假新闻降级到我们的日常链接中(因为它已经变得越来越荒谬)，任何像联合专利法院(UPC)这样的事情都是违宪的；但是律师事务所和“新闻”网站(为这些律师事务所工作)仍然否认最简单的事实，因为他们继续在联邦议院的另一场骗局之前散布毫无根据的乐观情绪。
战争就是和平，微软热爱Linux(为了点击和利润而使疯狂的思维正常化)真相之战(或者根据微软反垄断案件和比尔·盖茨证词中的许多例子，为微软收购竞争对手提供便利)正在如火如荼地进行，无数毫无根据的文章试图模糊微软和微软替代品之间的差距
IBM：“主人”这个词是粗鲁的(除了我们自己使用它的时候)，指出IBM(和Red Hat；)极力消除无伤大雅的词(主人这个词并不意味着奴隶的存在或奴隶制那样的关系)与IBM本身的言行不符。“主人”这个词是很粗鲁的(除了我们自己使用它的时候)，指出IBM(和Red Hat‘s)极力消除无伤大雅的词(主人这个词并不意味着奴隶的存在或奴隶制那样的关系)，这与IBM本身的言行不符。
note_en:
The most powerful and versatile Web browser ever to exist is becoming just an “app” with fewer compelling reasons to adopt it because today’s Firefox is less user-centric and more Mozilla-centric (with buzzwords, political pandering and marketing rather than technical substance)
  Summary: Mozilla’s business model keeps changing for the worse, as the “app” mentality and/or the “social control media” mindset are chosen over the  needs of actual (longtime) users, limiting the extensibility of the Firefox browser in the name of “performance” or “simplicity” (as if all that users need is “dark mode” and a choice of search engines)
 OVER the years we wrote  dozens of articles about Mozilla and Firefox, mostly congratulatory at the beginning (when releases were stable and infrequent), but in recent times we became more critical because Mozilla is no longer the same company. It’s vastly better than Microsoft, sure, but it’s getting worse — not better — over time. Long before the layoffs we already warned that the direction that had been taken was wrong. It served to alienate both developers and users — the very thing Mozilla relied on for over a decade. Even before DRM and ‘Eich-gate’ amongst other debacles there were issues associated with privacy, which is nowadays just empty rhetoric at Mozilla [ 1,  2] (or a form of marketing).
 Mozilla isn’t a GNU project; in fact, there are Firefox forks that are. We don’t suppose Mozilla will champion freedom to the extent GNU does, but that’s just not the point. Mozilla seems to have abandoned not only freedom but also developers and users. This is a suicidal path. Without momentum from  outside the company Mozilla might not be financially viable; it has long relied on an army of volunteers, both developers and ‘marketers’ (or advocates). Losing them isn’t an option, but Mozilla seems to have overlooked what actually made Firefox so popular in the first place.
  Daniel (‘Canta’)  recently wrote a decent article on this subject, translated/curated from Spanish by both myself and him.
 Earlier today I updated Mozilla Firefox, which I barely use anymore (I use a mix of Konqueror, Falkon and QupZilla on older machines). I am actually a bit horrified to find that this update or ‘upgrade’ (much newer version) made things worse in several ways.
 Some things can be ‘fixed’ by altering the settings, but some are not fixable. “While some DRM-controlled content can be viewed using the Adobe Flash plugin, many services are moving towards HTML5 video that requires a different DRM mechanism called a Content Decryption Module (CDM),” says the page Firefox directs me to. Embracing DRM did not help or save Mozilla, did it? It likely just alienated many people like myself, who used to advocate and recommend Firefox to people.
  Several usability problems became apparent when the ‘upgrade’ was done this morning. But there’s even worse stuff. When it was ‘upgraded’ to the latest ESR, Mozilla (likely  not the Debian packagers) had “Recommended by Pocket” toggled (on) by default, in effect spewing crap (mental noise/clutter) at me any time I opened a tab…
 It certainly feels like nowadays Mozilla treats Firefox like an  extension of the social control media mindset. It should instead combat/fight  back against it. But look who runs Mozilla now…  Microsoft and  Facebook executives.
 Once upon a time Mozilla appealed to geeks, who then recommended Firefox to friends, colleagues, and especially family (like kids and parents who were not necessarily passionate about computers and just clicked “the Internet”, which is what they called a blue “E”). Nowadays Mozilla fosters planned obsolescence for developers (I’ve made some contributions to Firefox in the extensions/themes sense), or ‘digital rot’ for plug-ins/extensions that worked just fine at one point (or for over a decade!). Firefox had more useful extensions in 2005 than it has in 2020 (I should know having embraced Firefox in 2004; I had used Netscape and Mozilla before that). XUL, for example, should not have been abandoned, but then again they care about money (paying their CEO over $2,000,000 per year,  plus bonuses) than users and volunteer developers.
 Mozilla may have worked fine for the bottom line of the current management team (millionaires), but it’s not working for many of us who need a “big browser” to challenge an increasingly proprietary monopoly/oligopoly in Web browsing. Other than Firefox, all the “big browsers” (that are widely supported and considered to be “must support”) are proprietary, usually with some openwashing slant.
 If Mozilla can no longer champion a free and open Web, and if Waterfox became an extension of the surveillance industry (same owners as  Startpage’s), then the whole Gecko family is becoming a lost cause or a losing strategy. Monoculture that revolves around GAFAM would make the Web  proprietary with DRM, necessitating a move to alternatives (to the Web itself, not just to Web browsers, as DRM is now incorporated into Web standards).
 Final note: while I agree with Mozilla’s political orientation on most issues, I’d appreciate  not having Mozilla’s words shoved down my throat (or up my head) every time I open a new tab. This kind of “UX” (User eXperience) is a hallmark of non-free software and it’s a symptom of what Mozilla is fast becoming. The user of the Web browser should be in full control of the browser, not having to rely on any third party to assess or rank or censor pages while pushing somebody’s editorialised messages. The browser should render pages, not be somebody’s billboard. Respect people’s judgment, resist the temptation to become a ‘net nanny’ (even at the  application level/layer).  █
  If you liked this post, consider  subscribing to the RSS feed or join us now at  the IRC channels.
            [Meme] Openwashed (and Spying) 'Killer App' for GNU/Linux? A Basic Windows Calculator!   There's no "Linux" news to cover more important and urgent than just another useless calculator (this one with notorious Microsoft telemetry) being ported to GNU/Linux
  Open-Sourcing the Investigation of Gates Deposition Tapes and Their Relationship to Current (Present) Affairs   The so-called 'philanthropist' and 'giver' that the media tells us Bill Gates suddenly became (he has earned another ~$10,000,000,000 since the pandemic began) needs further exposing because, having raided the software world (monopoly by law-breaking), he now does the same in other domains
 The Supposedly 'Soft' Bill Gates Interrogated About What Was Called His “Jihad”   We revisit the odd choice of words (repeatedly used by Mr. Gates internally), suggestive of some divine call for war/arms in the business context
 Why Microsoft Will Not Embrace GNU/Linux (to Replace Windows)   The Bill Gates deposition shows that Gates was aware Windows had become a market monopoly and sought to leverage that to push whatever “killer app” he thought would shield Microsoft’s monopoly; those tactics never changed (here’s an example from two days ago: “Microsoft is foisting Office web apps on Edge users”)
 The Sad Story of Mozilla Keeps Getting Sadder Because Mozilla's Managers Abandoned Users and Chose Companies as Their Clients   Mozilla's business model keeps changing for the worse, as the "app" mentality and/or the "social control media" mindset are chosen over the needs of actual (longtime) users, limiting the extensibility of the Firefox browser in the name of "performance" or "simplicity" (as if all that users need is "dark mode" and a choice of search engines)
     You May Need to Stop War to Stop Microsoft   "Today, corporations continue to lobby for more extreme copyright and fewer rights for public expression. The government favours the lobbyists, through a system that Lessig concedes (in a speech at Dartmouth University) is essentially legalised bribery."
 Tech is Hard, But Being Agnostic and Uncaring is Even Harder   People who become better informed about the hostile direction technology has taken in the past couple of decades would be wise to avoid most of it; communicating the dangers is very important for society's integrity in a time of unprecedented corporate coup (with "contact tracing" and other 'alternative medicine' prescribed in the name of 'health' and 'solidarity')
  [Meme] When Linux 'News' is Clickbait and Worse   We're once again seeing that things are overlooked by the media until Microsoft does something, in which case it's considered worth promoting
  A FIDO/FIDO2 False Sense of Security for Premium Prices   From the attack on software freedom (including Richard Stallman and other leaders/luminaries) we've seen a shift to attacks on privacy itself, e.g. auditable encryption; today we discuss the troubling developments in the FIDO/FIDO2 space
 ClearlyDefined is Just Microsoft Land Grab (Which the OSI Now Actively Participates in)   Today's OSI isn't a protector of the Free Software Definition or even the Open Source Definition; it's becoming little but a vessel serving Microsoft's agenda, centralising things for Microsoft to command and control
 Linus Torvalds on GNU When His Ego Was Still Smaller   More than two decades ago the creator of Linux explained the relation/connection of his project to GNU (which predates his project by almost a whole decade and also inspired him); nowadays he pretends GNU barely exists and the Linux Foundation pretends that GNU is just some part of Linux
  The Second Wave of GNU/Linux Adoption and Free Software Growth   Evidence suggests that more people are turning to Free software (there’s also a recent survey to that effect, published earlier this month) while proprietary software giants perish; the role played by the pandemic is measurable and proprietary software giants will struggle as it goes on and on
 Activism and Free Software   The fight for software freedom ought to go on; software used to be free before it was stolen from us, soon becoming a de facto private monopoly of barons who amass software patents and buy control over all the work (e.g. GitHub)
 Health Report of GNU and Linux News Sites in 2020 (a Pandemic Year)   The deterioration of sites that focus on GNU/Linux (news, instructional material, analysis etc.) is a growing problem; it's as if a lot of it became "private property" of monopolies, with many projects now indirectly controlled by Microsoft (through GitHub) while a cohesive community voice is systematically muzzled and citizen journalism seems to have lost momentum
   Transcripts of Bill Gates’ Lies: Part IV (Last)   Techrights is curating and maintaining plain text versions of the Gates deposition of 1998, in which years if not decades of the company's abuses were scrutinised in a face-to-face fashion
  Response to “Microsoft Shuts Down a Hacking [sic] Operation”   "I responded to electoral-vote.com because they mentioned Microsoft as the "good guy"," writes DaemonFC. "For taking down some ransomware (with lawyers, of course)."
 We've Relegated UPC 'Fake News' to Our Daily Links (Because It Has Gotten Increasingly Ridiculous)   Anything like the Unified Patent Court (UPC) is simply unconstitutional; but law firms and ‘news’ sites (working for these law firms) are still in denial about the simplest of facts as they continue spreading baseless optimism ahead of another Bundestag charade
 War is Peace, Microsoft Loves Linux (Normalising Demented Thinking for Clicks and Profit)   The war on truth (or facilitating Microsoft's takeover of its competition, as per many examples in the Microsoft antitrust cases and Bill Gates deposition) is in full swing, with countless/numerous baseless articles looking to blur the gap between Microsoft and the alternative to Microsoft
 IBM: The Word “Master” is Rude (Except When We Use It Ourselves)   Pointing out that IBM's (and Red Hat's) push to eliminate mostly innocuous words (the word "master" does not imply presence of slaves or a relationship like slavery) doesn't match the words and behaviour of IBM itself
title: 莫兹拉的悲伤故事愈演愈烈
title_en: The Sad Story of Mozilla Keeps Getting Sadder

大多数人都知道我是Python图书的作者、演讲者和培训师。可以肯定的是，我花了很多时间学习这门语言的奇特之处，深入研究它的高级功能，并就这些问题做了各种演示。然而，随着Python的成熟，它已经成长为一种大得多、复杂得多的语言。这样，我就越来越不可能掌握整个语言及其所有相关工具--我一直缺乏更新Python Essential Reference的动力，这一点变得更加明显。
作为一名著名的Python教师，总是有一定的压力要紧跟最新的发展，并引导人们走向一种合理的最佳实践。然而，如果我退后一步问问自己，这样做的最终目的是什么？我认为答案是Python只是一个解决问题和思考问题的工具。它只是一个这样的工具，但不是唯一的工具。因此，我决定优雅地退出Python世界，因为它让我担心Python本身的话题。
不管怎么说，我一直都是一个更有独立思想的研究者和学者。例如，我从来都不是核心开发人员，即使在我使用Python的最初几天，我也更像是一名精通多种语言的人--通过Swig项目与许多其他语言社区进行交互。当我还是一名大学教授时，我也没有把Python作为一个主题来教授，尽管我有时会在某些课上用到它。我目前的课程反映了我对许多大学兴趣的回归，以及超越特定编程语言选择的更广泛的主题。这真的是我想把精力花在这里的地方。
展望未来，我仍然希望花相当多的时间用Python编写代码。毕竟，我确实相当喜欢这门语言的基本核心。然而，我期待着简单地成为一个随意的Python用户，而不是感觉到持续不断的压力，让我的大脑围绕着每一个PEP。实际上，这听起来很不错。
另外，我最近完成了一本将在2021年出版的Python新书。它不会是一本全面的参考资料(抱歉，Python基本参考资料没有更新)，但我认为它仍然是解决日常问题的有用资源。敬请关注。
另外，我已经决定在知识共享下发布我的Python培训材料。实用Python编程可以在这里找到。“Python高级精通”即将面世。
[更多位]
note_en:
Most people know me as a Python book author, speaker, and trainer. To be sure, I've spent a lot of time learning the quirks of the language, diving into its advanced features, and giving various presentations on such matters. However, as Python has matured, it has grown into a substantially larger and vastly more complex language. With this, it has become increasing impossible for me to grasp the entirety of the language and all of its associated tooling--something made ever more clear by my ongoing lack of motivation to update the  Python Essential Reference.
  As a known teacher of Python, there has always been a certain pressure to stay on top of the latest developments and to steer people towards a kind of reasonable best practice. However, if I step back and ask myself "what is the ultimate purpose of this?", I think the answer is that Python is merely a tool for problem solving and thinking. It is only one such tool, but it is not the only tool. Thus, with that, I've decided to gracefully bow out of the Python world so far as it concerns me worrying about the topic of Python for its own sake.
  I've always been more of an independently minded researcher and academic anyways. For instance, I've never been a core developer and even in my earliest days with Python, I was more of a polyglot--interacting with many other language communities via the  Swig project. I also didn't teach "Python" as a topic when I was a university professor although I sometimes used it in certain classes. My current crop of  courses reflect a return to many of my university interests as well as a broader range of topics that transcend the choice of a specific programming language. This is really where I want to be spending my energy.
  Going forward, I still expect to spend a fair bit of time coding in Python. After all, I do rather like the basic core of the language. However, I'm looking forward to simply being a casual Python user as opposed to feeling a constant pressure to wrap my brain around every last PEP. Actually, that sounds pretty good.
    P.S. I recently finished what will become a new Python book in 2021. It won't be a comprehensive reference (sorry, no update to the Python Essential Reference), but I think it will still be a useful resource for day-to-day problem solving. Stay tuned.
  P.P.S. I've decided to release my Python training material under Creative Commons. "Practical Python Programming" can be found  here. "Advanced Python Mastery" will be coming soon.

好的，头衔是夸张的--实际上我和我的父母相处得很好--但是和我谈过这件事的绝大多数朋友和熟人似乎都对他们的父母怀有某种强烈的反感。我已经找过了，但是我找不到任何关于这个话题的数据(所以我可能碰上了一群不同寻常的憎恨父母的人)。
对于这种现象，我已经想出了一些可能的解释，但如果没有数据，这些解释几乎毫无用处，但无论如何，我还是要把它们写下来。如果憎恨父母是人类的普遍现象：
-恨你的父母只是人类发展的一部分(例如，否则你永远不会学会将自己与他们分开)，所以我们可以期待这是一种普遍现象。
-养育孩子真的很难，糟糕的养育方式真的是破坏性的，所以普通人的父母给他们造成了很大的伤害，所以有理由恨他们。
-随着经济停滞不前，父母的压力越来越大，他们两人都要工作，所以他们是更糟糕的父母，见上图。
-随着文化变革的加速，我们的父母对我们似乎越来越陌生-不像其他人，而更像是遥远的当局。显然，作为一个物种，我们并不热衷于专横的权威。
-通配符：当人们生活在几代人的家庭中时，你可以看到你的父母有多可怕，这样你就可以更容易地原谅你父母的错误。现在我们大多生活在核心家庭中，我们的父母犯的错误似乎是没有道理的。
我是不是完全误入歧途了？我是不是完全正确，再也不需要进行批判性思考了吗？
一级抽样偏差：如果你住的地方有很多迁徙的年轻人，如果与父母相处融洽的人倾向于呆在他们身边，而与父母关系不好的人想要离开很远，那么你会看到更多的是后者。
年龄范围：我和父母相处得很好，但肯定有几年我需要增加一些距离。作为一个年轻的成年人..。与那些仍然把你当孩子看待的人在一起可能会更难。当你年龄稍大一些时，这种情况就会缓解。时间线大致与孙辈可能进入等式的时间一致。
社会两极分化和过滤泡沫，也就是疯狂的叔叔效应：如果你的父母最终陷入了一个非常不同的过滤泡沫中，而且碰巧不是那种检查Snope的人，那么听到源源不断的城市神话来证明一些真正可怕的政策立场是合理的，这可能会令人沮丧。
从60年代或70年代以来，父母和孩子的年龄差距也一直在扩大。(在社会、道德、文化层面)与年长20岁的父母比年长35岁的父母建立关系可能容易得多。
第一级，那些对父母感到委屈的人会比那些和他们相处得很好的人更经常地谈论他们。
除非你随机抽样调查你的朋友关于他们父母的情况，否则这会歪曲你的数据。
水平1也很容易将任何个人缺点归咎于错误的育儿方式，无论是否确实存在任何因果关系。我个人不知道有多少人恨他们的父母。
二级流行弗洛伊德主义给了人们一个把一切都归咎于父母的借口，也给了人们一个晚年不回报照顾的借口。
第一级，在我看来，有两件事是孩子和父母之间日益增长的敌意的来源，而这两件事在最初的帖子中没有提到：
首先，在过去几代人的时间里，发达国家大部分地区的孩子独立出游的能力大幅下降，因此许多父母对许多孩子实质自由的控制变得更加强烈。换句话说，当你需要一辆车四处走动时，孩子们是不能的。
其次，政治派系(至少在美国)之间的反感情绪日益高涨，自由主义和保守主义之间存在代沟。我预计这预示着几代人之间的反感情绪会增加，因此一些孩子和他们的父母之间的反感情绪也会增加。
第二层次第一个因素是一个巨大的因素，大多数人在分析家庭生活的变化时，出于某种原因甚至没有提到这一点。
直到我和那些父母不好的人走得很近，我才意识到我的父母有多好。
我以为我父母的水平一般。可能有点压抑，而且不只是一点烦人。但是我已经意识到他们是(现在也是)优秀的父母。
当然，我知道有些父母虐待或疏忽。但我不明白，即使没有虐待或疏忽，亲子关系也能变得多么糟糕。我的一个好朋友没有受到虐待或忽视，但也没有受到严格的教育。另一个好朋友不过是他们父母争斗中的一个棋子。
朋友们会告诉我，哇，你的父母真棒！下次他们来的时候告诉我。我会想，什么，真的吗？现在我知道他们是对的了！
2级已通过。我认为这种观点与年龄和经验有关，当你低于一定年龄时，很难得出这种观点，因为很难分辨出，在你到了人生的某一阶段之前，谁的父母“真的”把他们搞砸了。我的母亲有很多精神问题，这无疑对我造成了一些伤害，但随着我年龄的增长，我意识到她给了我多少有力的工具来帮助我在生活中取得成功。虽然她和我父亲的婚姻不是最浪漫的，但他们相爱、稳固、可靠，这比我17岁时想象的要重要得多。我丈夫对他的父母也有类似的顿悟，他只是认为他们在他自己成年之前都是非常正直的。现在我们无法停止承认他们有多棒。
级别1我拒绝这个前提。我既爱我的父母，也喜欢我的大多数朋友。
第二级我认为OP对他的前提是错误的可能性相当开放，他更感兴趣的是为什么它是错误的。
第二层听起来我们都有一些趣闻轶事：(你碰巧知道有没有关于这个话题的调查数据？
当我去上大学的时候，我会说我和父母的关系很好，但不是很好。我和他们的关系一直很开放，会告诉他们一些事情，但老实说，我对父母的看法并不是最好的。我妈妈曾经告诉我，我让她觉得自己是个失败者，因为我不相信上帝。这特别刺痛，因为我觉得我是个很棒的孩子。学业上很成功，诸如此类。她很快就道歉了，但损害已经造成。我父亲比我母亲更虔诚，我责备他除了“相信上帝”和“努力工作”之外，在如何对待这个世界方面没有成为我更好的榜样。我把我缺乏社交技能归咎于他们，但后来意识到他们尽其所能把我赶出了我的圈子。我就是不肯让步。
在我大学二年级和三年级之间的那个暑假里，我顿悟到我的父母为他们的孩子做出了巨大的牺牲。我的母亲放弃了法律职业，呆在家里照顾我和我的兄弟姐妹，我的父亲为了得到升职来养活我们，一直在拼命当工程师。他们可能是来自相当古怪的父母的丁克尔伯格夫妇(打赌这是这部剧第一次被引用在这个子笑声中)，但他们却选择了我们。失声痛哭，最后打电话告诉他们我爱他们。
后来又以我自己的方式回到了宗教上。我可能低估了这方面的事情，但我觉得这并不是特别重要。我学到了更多关于父母教育的知识，因为我已经长大了，可以参加家庭聚会上的成年人谈话了。这让我更欣赏他们。我意识到我的父亲是一个多么伟大的榜样，在如何成为一名现在的父亲和如何成为我们社区的一名积极成员方面。我想说我们的关系比以往任何时候都要好。我现在25岁，搬回家住了几个月，期间我从上学的地方附近的工作过渡到离我长大的地方更近的工作。我会尽我所能让他们的生活更轻松。他们给了我一切，至少我能做的就是帮助照顾他们。
至于为什么其他人和他们的父母关系不好呢？我认为很大一部分原因是年龄和成熟度。我的很多朋友在高中时与父母的关系都不是很好。我想这只是成长的一部分。直到你搬出房子，你才会真正感激你的父母为你做了多少。每一位家长都会在他们没有教给你或没有帮助你的东西上有缺点。关键是要意识到他们可能已经尽了最大努力，至少在我成长的地方，他们做得非常好。有些人没有达到欣赏的程度，因为“父母应该做这些事情”，或者“他们这样做只是因为他们不得不这样做”。当时我对自己的未来进行了大量的自我反省(我一个人住在学校上暑期班，没有太多事情可做)，所以我想我比我的一些同龄人更早达到这一点。
除此之外，一些家长只是在责任方面做得不好。有时这是出于选择，优先考虑其他事情而不是他们的孩子，但有时环境也起到了一定作用。我想关键是要知道不同之处。
我要说的最后一件事是，由于现在大部分工作都在家里以外，大多数孩子都不知道他们的父母为养活他们付出了多大的努力。从小到大，我知道我的父亲每天都去办公室，有时还会出差，但直到我自己进入这个行业，意识到这是多么令人精疲力竭(在生孩子之前！)。我真的很感激他为了给我们提供更好的生活而在事业上做出的牺牲。我从小到大更欣赏我的妈妈，因为她在家工作，她帮我们准备上学，所以她所做的一切都是看得见的。
第一级我一直认为，大多数人倾向于憎恨限制他们想要的自由的权威人物，无论他们是否为人父母。
第一级我认为其中很大一部分(如果不是决定性的因素)是，我们社会的从属模式，例如在教育和职业培训方面，已经从从属于有血有肉的人，转变为从属于非人性化的官僚机构。
传统的可强制执行的师徒关系给许多人的印象是专横和过度控制，然而，这些人认为，在十几岁的时候，把醒着的每一分钟都花在塑造自己和他们的简历上，是一种取悦招生委员会的形式，招生委员会反过来会给他们机会承担数十万美元的债务，就为了成为一名医生或律师。如果说有什么不同的话，那就是这种现代形式的统治客观上更糟糕，但它一点也不被认为是专横或控制的。
这是为什么？这是因为传统上是一个真实的人控制着你，而现在它是一个无名的、无名的官僚机构。无论出于什么原因，我们文化上对支配和控制的厌恶只适用于那些看起来像人类而不是非人的代理人。另一个例子是，即使在做几乎完全相同工作的餐厅里，人们舒适地给服务员点菜，人们也会对命令家政佣人感到多么不舒服。前者是个人的，后者是非个人的。
这与憎恨自己的父母相联系的地方是，亲子关系传统上涉及到父母对孩子的非常个人化的掌握和指导，当然还包括对孩子需求的高度响应，以及依赖于人类判断和母性本能等高度个性化的育儿方法。在这种关系中，亲子关系传统上涉及到父母对孩子的非常个人化的控制和指导，当然还包括对孩子需求的高度响应，以及依赖于人类判断和母性本能的高度个性化的育儿方法。
在现代社会，这种关系是非常反文化的，虽然这种影响还不足以彻底瓦解家庭，但它的强大程度足以让父母对以一种真正深刻和持久的方式养育自己的孩子感到不舒服，就好像亲子关系是必要的恶而不是积极的善一样。反过来，当孩子达到父母不再是唯一的权威，更受更广泛的文化影响的阶段时，就会进一步鼓励他们远离任何真正深刻或持久的纽带，这些纽带可能意味着支配、控制、掌握或从属。这造成了你所描述的强烈反感。
在我看来，这是一种文化因素在起作用。基本上，我见过的每个美国人都恨他们的父母，但在我认识的其他荷兰人中，这种情况似乎非常罕见。有没有人能对其他文化中的这一点发表意见呢？
第二级找不到最初的研究，但这篇博客表明，即使在西方国家之间也有很大的差异。
友好关系在英国最为普遍，75%的父母表示与成年子女关系融洽。在西班牙，63%的人报告了积极的关系，在德国，49%，在美国，51%。
与生活在其他地方的家庭相比，美国家庭发生所谓的不和谐关系的可能性是其他地方的两倍多，即那些由强烈的负面情绪(如分歧和紧张)定义的家庭，没有任何强烈的积极情绪，包括亲密感和友好感。
第一级可能不会。我想不出有哪个时代是这样的。大多数传统文化实际上都是向父母“致敬”的。
在过去，你是由你的父母抚养长大的。现在你被学校系统、媒体和娱乐文化养大了。这其实并不夸张。孩子们花在这些事情上的时间比和父母在一起的时间还多。在早期，你实际上会和父母一起看电视，他们会选择频道(你能想象吗？)。在1920年，你会做什么，除了书籍、父母或邻居的孩子，几乎没有课外活动，没有家庭作业，你会做什么？在1800年，你很可能会被你的父母雇佣，由你的母亲抚养长大。
如今，父母的养育方式是过去的10%。养育意味着“与之共度时光”，正规的养育在历史上是新的。取而代之的是喝着咖啡因、积极进取的教师和工业媒体综合体。无论是好是坏，很大程度上取决于父母。
我们把我们的父母和我们在媒体上看到的理想化的父母进行比较。此外，考虑到网上关于“正确”育儿方法的丰富信息，更容易看到你父母的错误。
第二级，他们真的这么做了吗？我不是在反问。我认为我们读到的很多关于孝道的东西更多的是由父母写的，类似于我们现在所拥有的电视家庭与现实世界中的家庭区分。
note_en:
Ok, title is hyperbole - I actually get along pretty well with my parents - but a vast majority of my friends and acquaintances I've talked to about this seem to have some kind of strong antipathy towards their parents. I've looked, but I can't find any data on this topic (so I might just have run into an unusual group of parent-haters).
 I've come up with some possible explanations for this phenomenon that are pretty much useless without data, but I'm gonna write them down anyway. If hatred of parents is a human universal:
 -Hating your parents is just a part of human development (e.g., because otherwise you'd never learn to separate yourself from them) and so we can expect it to be a universal.
 -Parenting is really hard, and bad parenting is really destructive, so the average person has had a great deal of damage done by their parents, and so has reason to hate them.
   -Parents are under more and more stress as the economy stagnates and both of them have to work, so they're worse parents and see above.
  -As cultural change accelerates, our parents seem more and more alien to us - less like other people and more like distant authorities. Obviously, we're not big on arbitrary authorities, as a species.
 -A wildcard: when people lived in multi-generational households, you got to see how terrible your parents parents were, and so could more easily forgive your parents for their mistakes. Now that we mostly live as nuclear families, our parents' mistakes seem unjustifiable.
 Am I totally off base here? Am I totally right and don't need to do any more critical thinking ever again?
   level 1  Sampling bias: if you live somewhere with lots of migratory young people if the people who get on great with their parents tend to want to stay near them while people who do not get on with their parents want to get far away then you'll see more of the latter.
 Age range: I get on well with my parents but there were definitely a few years where I  needed to gain some distance. Being a young adult.... it can be harder to be around people who still view you as a child. When you get a bit older this eases off. Timelines roughly aligning with when grandchildren may be entering the equation.
 Polarisation in society and filter bubbles, aka the crazy uncle effect: If your parents ended up stuck in a very different filter bubble and happen to not be the kind of people who check snopes then it can be frustrating to hear a constant stream of urban myths justifying some really horrible policy position
 level 2  The parent-child age gap has also been  widening since the 60's or 70s. It's likely much easier easier to relate (on a social, moral, cultural level) to a parents 20 years older than a parent 35 years older.
  level 1  Someone who feels aggrieved by their parents will talk about them more often than people who get on with them fairly well.
 Unless you're talking to a random sample of your friends about their parents, this will skew your "data."
 level 1  It is also quite easy to blame any personal shortcomings on wrong parenting, no matter whether any causality actually exists. I personally don't know that many people that hate their parents.
 level 2  Pop freudism gave people an excuse to blame their parents for everything and an excuse to not return their care in old age.
 level 1  Two things stand out to me as sources of increased animosity between children and parent that weren't mentioned in the original post:
 First, the ability of children to go places on their own has  gone sharply down over the last few generations in large parts of the developed world, so the control many parents have over many of their children's substantive freedoms has become much more intense. To put it another way, when you need a car to get around, children can't.
 Second, there's  a trend of increasing antipathy between political factions (at least in the US) and a liberal/conservative generational divide. I'd expect that predicts increased antipathy between generations, and therefore between some children and their parents.
 level 2  The first one is a huge factor that most people don't even mention for some reason in their analysis of how family life has changed.
 level 1  I didn't realize how good my parents were until I became close with people with bad parents.
 I thought my parents were about average. A little oppressive, maybe, and more than a little annoying. But I've come to realize that they were (and are) excellent parents.
 I knew that some parents were abusive or negligent, of course. But I didn't understand how bad a parent/child relationship can be even without abuse or neglect. A good friend of mine wasn't mistreated or ignored, but wasn't exactly nurtured either. Another good friend was merely a pawn in a battle between their parents.
 Friends would tell me, "wow, your parents are great! Tell me the next time they visit." And I would think, "what, really?" Now I know they were right!
 level 2  Seconded. I think that perspective comes with age and experience, and it's hard to arrive at it when you're below a certain age, since it's hard to tell whose parents have *truly* screwed them until you get to a certain point in life. My mom had a lot of mental issues that definitely did me some damage, but as I've gotten older I've realized how many powerful tools she gave me to succeed in life. And while she and my dad didn't have the most romantic marriage, they were loving, solid and dependable and that counts for far more than I imagined at 17. My husband had a similar epiphany about his parents, who he just thought were terribly square until he was an adult himself. Now we just can't stop acknowledging how great they are.
  level 1  I reject the premise. I both love and like my parents, and so do most of my friends.
 level 2  I think OP is pretty open about the possibility that his premise is wrong, he's more interested in  why it's wrong.
   level 2  Sounds like we both have some anecdotes :( Do you happen to know if there's any survey data on this topic?
  level 1  When I left for college, I would say my relationship with my parents was good but not great. I’ve always been open in my relationship with them and telling them things, but truthfully, I didn’t have the best view of my parents. My mom once told me I made her feel like a failure because I didn’t believe in God. That especially stung because I felt I was a great kid. Academically successful, and whatnot. She apologized shortly after but the damage was done. My dad was more religious than my mom and I blamed him for not being a better role model for me, in terms of how to deal with the world, other than by “trusting God” and “working hard”. I blamed my lack of social skills on them, but later realized that they did all they could to push me out of my bubble. I just refused to budge.
 During the the summer between my sophomore and junior years of college, I had an epiphany about the enormity of the sacrifices my parents had made for their children. My mother had foregone a career in law to stay home and take of me and my siblings, and my dad had busted his ass continually as an engineer to get promoted to provide for us. They could’ve been the Dinkelbergs from Fairly Odd Parents (bet that’s the first time this show has been referenced on this sub lol) but instead chose us. Broke down in tears and ended up calling them to tell them I loved them.
 Later came back to religion on my own terms. I might be underrating that aspect of things, but I don’t feel it was especially important. Learned more about my parents upbringing, since I was old enough to be included in the adults conversations at family gatherings. This helped me appreciate them more. Realized what a great role model my dad was, in how to be a present father and how to be an active member of our community. I’d say our relationship is better than it’s ever been. I’m 25 now and moved back home for a few months while I transitioned from a job near where I went to school to a job closer to where I grew up. I do pretty much anything I can to make their lives easier. They gave me everything, and the least I can do is help take care of them.
 As for why other people don’t have great relationships with their parents? I think a big part of it is age and maturity. A lot of my friends didn’t have the best relationships with their parents in high school. I imagine that’s just part of growing up. Until you move out of the house, you don’t really appreciate how much your parents do for you. Every parent is going to have shortcomings in something that they didn’t teach you, or help you with. The key is to realize that they probably did the best they could, and at least for where I grew up, they did tremendously. Some people don’t reach that point of appreciation because “parents are supposed to do these things” or “they only did that because they had to”. I was doing a lot of soul searching about my future at the time (I was living alone at school taking summer classes and didn’t have much to do), so I think I reached that point earlier than some of my peers.
 Beyond that, some parents are just bad at their responsibilities. Sometimes that’s by choice and prioritizing other things over their kids, but sometimes circumstances play a role too. I think it’s key to know the difference.
 The last thing I’ll say is that since most work is now outside of the home, most kids don’t have a clue how hard their parents work to provide for them. Growing up, I knew my dad went into the office everyday and sometimes travelled for work, but until I entered the same industry myself and realized how exhausting it was (before having kids!) that I truly appreciated the sacrifices he made with his career in order to provide a better life for us. I appreciated my mom more growing up because she worked from home and she helped us get ready for school, so everything she did was visible.
 level 1  I've always assumed that most people tend to hate authority figures that restrict freedoms they want, regardless of whether they're parents.
 level 1  I think a big piece of this, if not the decisive factor, is that the mode of subordination in our societies, e.g. in education and in the training of people for careers, has shifted from subordination to flesh-and-blood human beings to subordination to impersonal bureaucratic institutions.
 A traditional enforceable master-apprentice relationship strikes many people as domineering and overly controlling, yet these same people think nothing of devoting every waking moment during their teenage years to molding themselves and their "resumes" into a form that will please admissions committees who will in turn give them the chance to take on hundreds of thousands of dollars in debt just to become e.g. a physician or a lawyer. If anything this modern form of such domination is objectively worse, yet it's not perceived as at all domineering or controlling.
 Why is this? It's because traditionally an actual person dominated you, whereas now it's a nameless faceless bureaucracy. For whatever reason our cultural aversion to domination and control only applies to agents that seem to be human as opposed to inhuman. Another example of this is how uncomfortable people are with ordering domestic servants around even when they comfortably give orders to waiters in restaurants that are doing almost exactly the same work. The former is personal, the latter impersonal.
 Where this ties into hating your own parents is that the parent-child relationship traditionally involved very personal mastery and guidance of the child by the parent, coupled of course with great responsiveness to the child's needs, and highly individualized approaches to child-rearing that relied upon the likes of human judgment and maternal instincts.
 This kind of relationship is very counter-cultural in modern society, and while this effect isn't strong enough to outright dissolve families, it's strong enough to make parents uncomfortable with raising their own children in a way that produces a truly deep and lasting bond, as if the parent-child bond is a necessary evil rather than a positive good. In turn when the child reaches the stage where parents are no longer the sole authority and is more affected by the broader culture there is further encouragement to draw away from any truly deep or lasting bond that might imply domination, control, mastery, or subordination. This creates the "strong antipathy" you describe.
 level 1  To me, it feels like there's a cultural element at work. Basically every American I've ever met hates their parents, but this seems very rare among the other Dutch people I know. Can anyone weigh in on how that is in other cultures?
 level 2  Couldn't find the original study, but this blog indicates there is wide variance even between Western countries.
  Amicable relationships were most prevalent in England, with 75 percent of parents reporting harmonious ties with their grown-up kids. In Spain, 63 percent reported positive relationships, in Germany, 49 percent, and in the United States, 51 percent.
 American families were more than twice as likely as those living anywhere else to have so-called disharmonious relationships, or those defined by strong negative feelings, such as disagreement and tension, without any strong positive feelings, including feelings of closeness and amicability.
 level 1  Probably not. I can’t think of any era where it was common. Most traditional cultures actually “honored” their parents.
   In the past you were raised by your parents. Now you are raised by the school system, the media, and entertainment culture. This is not actually hyperbole. Kids spend more time on this stuff than with their parents. In earlier eras you would actually watch TV with your parents, and they chose the channels (can you imagine?). In 1920 what would you do, with no options but books, parents, or neighborhood kids, with few after school programs, with no homework? In 1800 you would likely be employed by your parents and raised by your mum.
 Today parents are doing 10% of the rearing they did in the past. Rearing meaning “time spent with”, formalized rearing is historically new. They have been replaced by caffeinated and motivated teachers and the industrial media complex. For better or for worse largely depends on parents.
 We’re comparing our parents to the idealized parents we find in media. Also, it’s easier to see the errors of your parents given the wealth of information available online regarding “proper” parenting methods.
 level 2  Did they actually? I'm not asking rhetorically. I think a lot of the "filial piety" stuff we read about was more of a "what-ought-to-be" written by the parents; something analogous to the TV family vs. real-world family distinction we have now.
title: 为什么每个人都恨他们的父母？
title_en: Why Does Everybody Hate Their Parents?

note:
莱克斯·弗里德曼询问微软首席技术官凯文·斯科特，如何才能经营一家拥有3万名工程师的公司。
雄心勃勃、聪明的工程师可以去任何地方。你如何激励他们？你怎么让他们留下来呢？
而且一直都是这样的。不仅对工程师，而且对整个人类都是如此。
尤瓦尔·哈拉里(Yuval Harari)在他的“智人”(Sapiens)一书中解释说，人类之所以特别，是因为他们生活在一个由自己的想法和客观现实创造的世界里。
他称这是我们的虚构现实。像钱、国家、公司--这些都是我们发明的。
以像黄金这样的东西为例。你不能吃黄金。你甚至不能用黄金做武器，因为它太软了。
因此，作为人类，我们拿出一些没有固有价值的东西，比如黄金，然后围绕它讲一个故事。我们构建叙事。
想想你每天给自己讲的故事。其中有多少是基于您的客观现实？
相反，可能的情况是，你的大脑全神贯注于哈拉里所说的虚构现实。
故事是我们作为一个物种进化的核心。碰巧的是，这也适用于公司。
史蒂夫·乔布斯是一位伟大的故事家，因为他专注于为什么而不是什么。
以1984年麦金塔电脑的广告为例。直到最后几秒钟才提到广告内容。
经常被忽视的是，同样的原则也被用来吸引苹果最优秀的人才。
“这就是什么是领导力。领导力就是有一个愿景，能够清晰地表达出来，这样你周围的人就能理解它，并就一个共同的愿景达成共识。“。
我以前写过这方面的文章，但是乔布斯会这样做的一种方式是创建一个现实扭曲场。
现实扭曲的领域混合了乔布斯的魅力，他强烈的成功意志，以及渴望歪曲任何事实以适应手头目标的渴望。
这就是他如何让他的团队在疯狂的最后期限前完成任务，以及他如何说服最优秀的人与他合作。
让大多数人向你推介他们的想法，听起来可能真的很复杂。
阅读大多数企业使命声明。你可能会想“那又怎么样？”
但当谈到领导团队时，与拥有清晰的愿景和能够讲故事相比，这两个因素都相形见绌。
这就是凯文·斯科特每天激励他的3万名工程师起床的方式。
有些人称它为时事通讯，我称它为美好时光。我写的是技术职业，以及你如何在你的职业生涯中取得成功。这是我最好的内容(就像这个案例研究)，每周向您提供一次。
note_en:
Lex Friedman asked Microsoft CTO, Kevin Scott, what it takes to run a company of 30,000 engineers.
 Ambitious, smart engineers can go anywhere. How do you motivate them? How do you make them stay?
   And it’s always been like this. Not just for engineers, but for humans in general.
     In his book Sapiens, Yuval Harari explains that humans are special because they inhabit a world created from their own ideas in addition to their objective reality.
 He calls this our  fictional reality. Things like money, nations, corporations - these are all things that we  invented.
 Take something like gold, for example. You can’t eat gold. You can’t even make weapons out of gold because it’s too soft.
 So as humans, we take something with no inherent value like gold and we tell a story around it. We construct the narrative.
     Think about the stories you tell yourself everyday. How much of them are based on your objective reality?
 Instead it’s probably the case that your mind is preoccupied by what Harari refers to as fictional reality.
 Stories are central to our evolution as a species. And it just so happens that this also holds true for companies.
  Steve Jobs was a great storyteller because he focused on the  why over the  what.
  Take  this commercial for the 1984 Macintosh. Not until the last few seconds is there even a mention of what’s being advertised.
     What often goes unnoticed is that these same principles are used to attract the best people at Apple.
  “ And that’s what leadership is. Leadership is having a vision, being able to articulate that so the people around you can understand it, and getting a consensus on a common vision.”
  I’ve  written about this before, but one of the ways Jobs would do this is by creating a Reality Distortion Field.
 The reality distortion field was a mix of Jobs’ charisma, his strong will to succeed, and an eagerness to bend any fact to fit the purpose at hand.
 It’s how he got his team to meet crazy deadlines and how he convinced the best to work with him.
  Ask most people to pitch you their idea and it’ll probably sound really complicated.
  Read most corporate mission statements. You’ll probably come away thinking “So what?”.
  But when it comes to leading a team, both those factors pale in comparison to having a clear vision and being able to tell a story.
 And that’s how Kevin Scott motivates his 30,000 engineers to get out of bed everyday.
        Some people call it a newsletter - I call it a good time. I write about tech careers and how you can get ahead in yours. It’s my best content (like this case study) delivered to you once a week.
title: 讲故事的力量

note:
最近的壮举，比如AlphaGo战胜了世界上最好的围棋选手，把强化学习(RL)带到了聚光灯下。然而，什么是RL，它是如何取得如此显著的效果的呢？
在第一篇文章中，我们将探讨蒙特卡罗控制方法(不是深度控制方法)，尽管该方法非常简单，但它是构建一些最先进的RL的基础。
RL问题包括(至少)两个实体：代理和环境，如下图所示。环境给予代理一种状态(也称为观察)。然后，代理根据提供的状态选择操作，并将其应用于环境。然后，环境通过给代理奖励(该操作的分数)来回应该操作。
例如，考虑一个孩子(代理)第一次玩游戏(环境)。孩子首先看到包含所有元素(状态)的游戏屏幕，然后决定要采取的行动。游戏给他打分(奖励)，过程重复到游戏结束(我们认为环境有明确的终止插曲)。经过足够的重复之后，孩子将开始理解他的行为如何影响环境，并(假设他是一个好胜心强的孩子)选择能使他的分数最大化的行为。
RL代理试图执行完全相同的操作。然而，与人类儿童不同的是，计算机还不具备天生的智能。那么，计算机如何学习最好的动作应该是什么呢？对于文本的其余部分，我们将提取这个问题，并重新推导出提供解决方案的算法。
蒙特卡罗学习是RL的一个子域，非常适合于求解有限(有限状态数)的马尔可夫决策过程(MDP)。MDP只是一类问题，对于这些问题，当前状态的知识提供了足够的信息来决定要采取的最佳行动。国际象棋就是一个可以被框定为MDP的问题的例子。任何时间点的板状态都包含确定下一步操作所需的所有信息。
让我们考虑一下下面描述的问题。我们有一个家用地板清洁机器人(清洁剂)，它的电池可以是低电量的，也可以是高电量的。机器人可以是清洁的，也可以是对接的。因此，机器人的环境由4种可能的状态组成：{清洁，高}，{清洁，低}，{停靠，高}，{停靠，低}。机器人控制器总是在3个动作中进行选择：清洁、停靠或等待，环境会为代理的动作提供奖励。然而，由于我们的电池传感器不精确，我们的机器人可能会迷路，所以我们的转换是随机的(本质上是概率的)。
例如，如果机器人当前处于电池电量较高的清洁状态，并选择继续清洁，则有80%的机会获得1的奖励并在相同状态下继续清洁。在这一点上，引入一些符号变得很重要。我们将给定先前状态s和动作a的新状态s‘的概率(从0到1)表示为：P(s’，r|s，a)。因此，我们可以将开场白写成：P({CLEVISH，HIGH}，1|{CLEVISH，HIGH}，CLEAN)=0.8。
通过检查来自每条路径的预期回报，可以猜测该环境的最佳解决方案可能是下面所示的蓝色路径的形式。
但是，在与环境交互之前，代理没有关于环境概率的信息。那么，我们如何才能培训一名座席在这种环境下操作呢？换句话说：我们如何找到代理可以遵循的策略(表示为π)(将状态映射到操作)，从而最大化总回报？
如果我们从一个简单地随机选择操作的代理开始会怎么样？下面是这样一个代理在包含50个步骤(其中前10个步骤如下所示)的插曲中运行的结果示例：
S0：停靠，高A0：等待R1：0 S1：停靠，高A1：等待R2：0 S2：停靠，高A2：等待R3：0 S4：停靠，高A4：等待R5：0 S5：停靠，高A5：清洁R6：1 S6：清洁，低A6：清洁R7：-3 S7：停靠，低A7：清洁R8：-3 S8：停靠，低A8：清洁R9：-3 S9：停靠，低...。
我们怎样才能利用我们刚刚收集到的信息呢？首先，我们可以使用表格表示此数据。其中行将对应于状态，列将对应于动作。这些条目将是在当前操作策略下处于给定状态时采取给定行动的加权平均预期回报。用某种符号表示：Q(s，a​)←Q(s​，a​)+α(G​−Q(s​，a​)。其中，α是我们对平均值的加权因子，通常被称为学习率，因为阿尔法越大意味着网络学习的速度越快，因为最近的观察结果有更大的影响。下面的表格称为Q表。
如果我们重复这个过程足够多的剧集，我们预计我们的表格会趋向于实际的回报。然而，对于足够大的问题，这种随机探索可能需要太长时间才能收敛。如果我们使用表中已有的信息来集中我们的探索，会怎么样？我们并不总是选择随机的操作，而是根据表中的当前值选择可以最大化回报的操作(称为贪婪操作)。在这种建议的方法下，当运行第2集时，如果代理处于清理高状态，它将始终选择停靠。我们从上一节建议的解决方案中了解到，这可能不是最佳解决方案。这表明，这种新的方法可能容易受到局部极小值的影响。如果我们从随机探索开始，但随着时间的推移(随着收集的数据越来越多，我们的Q表趋向于更好地逼近预期收益)，我们会变得更加贪婪吗？
最后一个想法是𝜀-贪婪算法背后的概念，我们在表中选择以1-𝜀+𝜀/n最大化预期回报的操作，以及不以𝜀/n概率最大化回报的值。我们希望在开始时探索更多(当我们的Q表不太可能很好地表示回报时)，并随着我们的近似随着剧集数量的增加而改进，缩小我们的探索范围。实现这一点的一种方法是使𝜀随着剧集数量的增加而衰减(变得更小)，但受到某些界限的限制，以便我们的算法永远不会完全贪婪，例如𝜀←MIN(𝜀*𝜀_Decen^num_epsides，𝜀_MIN_Bound)。这个概念通常被称为无限探索的贪婪(Glie)。使用这个算法，运行1000集，我们最终得到了下面的Q表。
观察基于此表选择贪婪操作如何成为我们的最佳(蓝色)策略，如前面的MDP图所示。
我们推导了一种简单的基于蒙特卡罗控制的强化学习算法。然而，如果我们试图解决的问题不是间歇性的，而是一个持续性的任务(一个没有明确结束的任务)，该怎么办？在下一篇文章中，我们将探索对这类问题有用的时差(TD)方法。
Udacity的深度强化学习课程为强化学习提供了坚实的介绍。
可以在这里找到示例中用于生成Q表的脚本。
note_en:
Recent feats such as AlphaGo’s victory over the world’s best Go player have brought reinforcement learning (RL) to the spotlight. However, what is RL and how does it achieve such remarkable results?
 In this first article, we will explore the Monte Carlo Control Method (not the deep kind) which, despite being elegantly simple, is the basis upon which some of the most advanced RL is built.
  RL problems consist of (at least) 2 entities: The  agent and the  environment, as shown in the figure below. The environment gives the agent a  state (also called an  observation). The agent then chooses an  action based on the provided state and applies it to the environment. The environment then replies to the action by giving the agent a  reward (a score for the action).
  For example, consider a kid (the agent) playing a game (the environment) for the first time. The kid starts by seeing the game screen containing all its elements (the state) and decides on an action to take. To which the game scores him (the reward) and the process reprises until the game ends (we consider environments with a clear termination  episodic). After enough repetitions, the kid will start to understand how his actions influence the environment and (assuming he is a competitive child) choose the actions that maximizes his score.
 An RL agent, attempts to do exactly the same. However, unlike a human child, computers don’t yet possess innate intelligence. So how does the computer learn what the best actions should be? For the remainder of the text we will distill this problem and re-derive an algorithm that provides a solution.
  Monte Carlo Learning is a subfield of RL that is well suited for solving finite (limited number of states) Markov Decision Processes (MDPs). An MDP is just a class of problems for which knowledge of the current state provides sufficient information to decide on an optimal action to take. Chess is an example of a problem that can be framed as an MDP. The board state at any point in time contains all the information required to determine the next action.
 Let’s consider the problem depicted below. We have a household floor cleaning robot (the agent) that has a battery that can be either LOW or HIGH in charge. The robot can be either CLEANING or DOCKED. Thus, the robot’s environment consists of 4 possible states: {CLEANING, HIGH}, {CLEANING, LOW}, {DOCKED, HIGH}, {DOCKED, LOW}. The robot controller always chooses between 3 actions: CLEAN, DOCK or WAIT, and the environment provides the agent with a reward for its action. However, since our battery sensor is not precise and our robot may get lost, our transitions are stochastic (probabilistic in nature).
  For instance, if the robot is currently in the state of CLEANING with HIGH battery and chooses to continue cleaning it has an 80% chance of receiving a reward of 1 and continuing on the same state. At this point, it becomes important to introduce some notation. We express a probability (from 0 to 1) of a new state  s′ and reward  r given a previous state  s and action  a as:  p(s′,r | s,a). Therefore, we could write our opening sentence as:  p({cleaning,high},1 | {cleaning,high},clean)=0.8.
 By inspection of the expected rewards from each path, it is possible to guess that an optimal solution to this environment is likely to be in the form of the blue paths shown below.
  However, the agent has no information about the environment’s probabilities prior to interacting with the environment. So how can we train an agent to operate on this environment? In other words: How do we find a  policy (represented as  π) that the agent can follow (that maps a state to an action) such that it maximizes total reward?
  What if we start with an agent that simply chooses actions at random? Below is an example of the results from such an agent running over an episode consisting of 50 steps (the first 10 of which are shown below):
 S0: Docked, High A0: Wait R1: 0 S1: Docked, High A1: Wait R2: 0 S2: Docked, High A2: Wait R3: 0 S4: Docked, High A4: Wait R5: 0 S5: Docked, High A5: Clean R6: 1 S6: Cleaning, Low A6: Clean R7: -3 S7: Docked, Low A7: Clean R8: -3 S8: Docked, Low A8: Clean R9: -3 S9: Docked, Low ...
 How can we make use of the information we have just collected? To start, we can represent this data using a table. Where the rows will correspond to the states and the columns to actions. The entries would be the weighted-average expected return from taking the given action while in the given state under the current operating policy. With some notation:   Q(s,a​)← Q(s​,a​)+ α( G​− Q(s​,a​)). Where α is our weighting factor for the average and is often called the  learning rate since a large alpha means the network learns faster as recent observations have a greater impact. The table below is termed a  Q-table.
  If we repeat this process for enough episodes we would expect our table to tend towards the actual returns. However, for sufficiently large problems this random exploration can take too long to converge. What if we use the information already in the table to focus our exploration? Instead of always picking random actions we pick the actions that would maximize our return (termed  greedy actions) based on the current values in the table. Under this proposed method, when running episode 2 if the agent were in the cleaning-high state it would always opt to dock. We know from the suggested solution in the previous section that this is likely not an optimal solution. It suggests, that this new approach may be susceptible to local minimas. What if we start with random exploration but over time (as more data is collected and our Q-table tends towards better approximations of the expected returns) we become more greedy?
 This final idea is the concept behind the  𝜀-greedy algorithm where we pick the action in the table that maximizes our expected return with  1 — 𝜀 + 𝜀/n and the values that don’t maximize our return with  𝜀/n probability. We want to explore more in the beginning (when our Q-table is unlikely to be a good representation of the rewards) and narrow our exploration as our approximations improves with a larger number of episodes. A method to achieve this is to have an 𝜀 that decays (becomes smaller) with increasing number of episodes but is limited by some bound so that our algorithm is never fully greedy such as  𝜀 ← min(𝜀 * 𝜀_decay^num_episodes, 𝜀_min_bound). This notion is often referred to as  greedy in the limit with infinite exploration  (GLIE). Using this algorithm and running for 1000 episodes we end up with the Q table below.
  Observe how choosing greedy actions based on this table is our optimal (blue) policy as shown earlier in the MDP figure.
  We have derived a simple reinforcement learning algorithm based on Monte Carlo Control. However, what if the problem we are attempting to solve is not episodic but instead a continuing task (one that has no clear end)? In the next article we will explore Temporal-Difference (TD) Methods that are useful for this class of problems.
  Udacity’s course in deep reinforcement learning provides a solid introduction to reinforcement learning.
 The scripts used to generate the Q table in the example can be found  here.
title: (浅？)。强化学习

我喜欢“谷歌软件工程”一书中的这句话。我觉得这是个很有帮助的提醒。
早些时候，我们断言“代码是负债，而不是资产”。如果这是真的，为什么我们在这本书的大部分时间里都在讨论构建可以使用几十年的软件系统的最有效的方法呢？为什么要把所有的精力都花在创建更多的代码上，而这些代码最终只会出现在资产负债表的负债端呢？代码本身并不带来价值：带来价值的是它提供的功能。如果满足用户需求，该功能就是一种资产：实现该功能的代码只是实现该目的的一种手段。如果我们可以从一行易维护、易理解的代码中获得与10,000行令人费解的意大利面条代码相同的功能，我们会更喜欢前者。代码本身是有成本的-代码越简单，同时保持相同数量的功能就越好。
note_en:
I loved this quote from the book  Software Engineering at Google. Found it a helpful reminder.
 Earlier we made the assertion that “code is a liability, not an asset.” If that is true, why have we spent most of this book discussing the most efficient way to build software systems that can live for decades? Why put all that effort into creating more code when it’s simply going to end up on the liability side of the balance sheet? Code itself doesn’t bring value: it is the functionality that it provides that brings value. That functionality is an asset if it meets a user need: the code that implements this functionality is simply a means to that end. If we could get the same functionality from a single line of maintainable, understandable code as 10,000 lines of convoluted spaghetti code, we would prefer the former. Code itself carries a cost — the simpler the code is, while maintaining the same amount of functionality, the better.
title: 代码是负债，而不是资产
title_en: “Code is a liability, not an asset”

note_en:
MBScript the scripting system for the game creation tool  Modbox. It was designed instead of using a existing text scripting language (like Lua/Javascript) for a few reasons:
 To allow for drag and drop editing without a keyboard. In VR \ AR, with Touch on a mobile device, or with a Gamepad.
 Designed from the start to work online. All code runs online automatically so designers never have to think about networking code.
 All changes made to MBScript code are also synced online to other players in real time - and can be edited while playing.
 Uses the same C# type system that Modbox is built with. The type system is used to show the correct options while editing visual scripting.
 Unlike node based visual scripting (like Blueprints / Playmaker), MBScript can be switched from visual mode to text mode at any time. A integrated VSCode like text editor (Monaco) is included with Modbox.
 Code can be copied and pasted between the node based  Wiring system, which uses MBScript
 It's event based - with events being any variable changes or ingame events on any entity in the world - so MBScript code can be easily hooked into by other scripts / mods.
  Here a MBScript asset is created to have the Entity 'Die' on Collision if the collision speed is over the 'Min Speed to Break' number variable. It also Prints the name of the Entity it collided with.
  MBScript assets can be created with the Edit Entity Desktop Window or the Edit VR Tool. Each asset creates a new   Entity Component   Entity Component - Entity Components are on Entities and set their behavior and other properties such as physics / how they look. New components can be made in either MBScript or C# programming type. MBScript Assets can be included in Entity   Prefab   Prefab - Prefabs are entity assets created ingame out of 1 or more other entities. Prefabs can then be shared online / between creations. (There is also Unity's Prefab system used in the ModboxSDK, which is different but same functionality).
 MBScript can be edited/run at anytime (Running in edit mode can be useful for creating Editor tools / operations. Editing in play mode is a easy way to test changes.)
 MBScript is also used to create  Game Managers - which are MBScript assets that are added to the world automatically, and easily accessed by other scripts (like  Singletons )
    MBScript code runs on  Events, which allows for running code based on any event happening on the Entity or in the world (such as a variable changes value, or a world event happens like a player joins).
  The 'On' lines add  Event Lines to run code on events. You can then add other code line types to set values / do operations:
 ForEach - Run code on a list. “Do this to everything in this set". Can run ‘ForEach Entity in the world change it’s material to Wood’
 After - Run code after a set amount of time. There are a few other line types: ‘ Local’ for temporary variables,  Return to stop running code and return from an event,  Repeat to do a loop for a set number of times.
    After adding a code line you use the dropdowns to choose a variable or set a value.
    The ‘+’ at the end of the line can be used to continue the line with another operation. So because ‘Contains’ returns a True/False, you can then hit the ‘+’ button and select ‘IsFalse’ to invert that return value:
  You can keep adding operations like this. Here the ‘Y’ part of the entity’s scale is selected, and set to the same Y value plus a random value:
        Components on the Entity and their variables (like it's Name in the Main component, or Mass in the Physics component)
          To change a variable value the Set line can be used to set to a new value (with "Set X to Y"). It can also be useful to do operations directly on the variable:
   The 'ForEach' line can be used to iterate through lists - like the 'All Entities' list in the world.
     Code lines can be dragged by moving the drag handle. Multiple lines can be selected and moved at once. Variables and values can also be dragged to copy them.
 Right click can be used to copy and paste MBScript (which just copies it to the Clipboard as text)
  At any time you can switch to text mode to edit, with autocomplete for variable names and immediate error updates.
 Here is the same script switched to text mode and edited, checking if the Entity has a 'Primitive Component' set on it, and if it does it prints the value of the Entity's   Physics Material Asset   Physics Material Asset - Physics Material Asset sets a Entity physics properties - including it's mass, it's Physics Sound Type, and on damage/collision effects :
title: Modbox可视化脚本语言

note:
这是科技界活跃的一周，Facebook、Twitter和苹果发布了重大新闻。但在占据新闻头条的喧嚣之后，对独角兽公司或价值10亿美元或以上的私营公司来说，看涨消息源源不断。
交易所花了一周的大部分时间研究独角兽公司或很快就会符合条件的公司的不同故事，令人惊讶的是，即使在我们要写的东西之外，也有这么多积极的金融消息可供选择。
例如，Databricks在定期发布之前向TechCrunch披露了一系列财务数据，包括到2020年第三季度末，其年运行率(而不是ARR)从2019年第二季度的2亿美元增加到3.5亿美元。它基本上已经为IPO做好了准备，但并不急于上市。
坚持我们的主题，Calm想要更多的钱来获得巨大的新估值，也许高达22亿美元，这并不令人惊讶。这更是独角兽的好消息。正如本周发布的“印度Razorpay(在新一轮1亿美元融资后成为)独角兽”的报道一样。
RazorPay只是新冠肺炎期间成为独角兽的众多印度初创企业之一。(这是本周发布的另一份摘要，内容是关于六家初创公司在“大流行”期间成为独角兽公司的情况。)。
最近有足够多的独角兽好消息，以至于我们都忘了这一切。像地震这样的事情筹集了9200万美元，将其估值从几周前推高至16亿美元。这是怎么在混搭中迷失的呢？
所有这些都很重要，因为尽管IPO市场在上个季度左右吸引了很多关注，但独角兽世界并没有停滞不前。的确，它感觉独角兽风投活动是我们自2019年以来看到的最高水平。
而且，正如我们稍后将看到的，就在我们说话的同时，独角兽磨坊的格栅正在重新装满。因此，在一些实质性的东西打破我们目前的投资和退出模式之前，预计会有更多相同的事情发生。
独角兽吃什么？现金。在过去的七天里，很多风投公司筹集了资金。
下面是部分列表。这可能是因为投资者希望在选举之前锁定新的资金，无论随之而来的是什么混乱。因此，以下是新富起来的人，顺序不分先后：
OpenView 4.5亿美元，Canaan 8亿美元，True Ventures 8.4亿美元，Lead Edge Capital 9.5亿美元。
一家名为本森资本合伙公司(Benson Capital Partners)的公司成立了一只5000万美元的基金。盖尔·本森(Gayle Benson)，该公司以他的名字命名，根据福布斯的说法，他拥有几支新奥尔良运动队。
此外，根据FundsglobalMENA的数据，由两名前500 Startups Mena投资者创建的Plus Venture Capital已经筹集了6000万美元。
第一轮正在寻找2.2亿美元，前谷歌高管李开复的华锐风投正在寻找10亿美元，而科斯拉想要更多一点。
所有这些资金都需要投入到工作中，这意味着对许多初创公司来说，需要更多的融资。交易所本周还赶上了一家有点新的公司：Race Capital。该公司由阿尔弗雷德·庄(Alfred Chuang)执掌，他的前身是BEA，现在是一名天使投资者，负责自己的基金，有5000万美元可投资。
就目前而言，坚持对初创企业的私人投资，本周发生了相当多的事情，我们需要了解更多。例如，API驱动的Argyle从贝恩资本风险投资公司(Bain Capital Ventures)筹集了2000万美元，用于FinLedger所说的“解锁和民主化就业记录的访问”。TechCrunch目前正在跟踪API主导的初创企业的进展情况。
金融科技方面，M1财经C系列为消费者金融科技平台融资4,500万美元，另一家机器人顾问公司WealthSimple融资8,700万美元，同时成为独角兽。当我们还在金融科技的水桶里的时候，斯利普本周为尼日利亚的初创公司Paystack投资了2,000万美元。我们需要更多地关注非洲的创业场景。在金融科技较小的一端，羊驼又筹集了1,000万美元，帮助其他公司成为罗宾汉。
在我们改变策略之前，还有一些其他的注意事项。由于远程教育的繁荣，卡胡特筹集了2.15亿美元，作为更大的教育技术热潮的一部分，2020年又是一个不可避免的趋势(我们自己的娜塔莎·马斯卡伦哈斯报道)。
从私人市场转到公共市场，我们不得不谈一谈SPAC。本周，交易所与Shift的托比·罗素(Toby Russell)通了电话。Shift现在是一家上市公司，在与SPAC合并后进行交易，即保险收购公司(Insurance Acquisition Corp.)。早期交易进行得很顺利，但这位首席执行官向我们详细介绍了他追求SPAC的确切原因，这实际上很有趣：
罗素表示，Shift本可以通过IPO上市，但优先考虑由SPAC牵头的首次公开募股，因为他的公司希望优化融资，以保持公司的增长。
怎么会这样？SPAC期权附带的私募股权投资(PIPE)确保了这一转变将拥有数亿现金。
Shift还希望将首席执行官所说的市场风险降至最低。无论更广泛的市场在做什么，SPAC交易都可能发生。随着该公司在4月份选择通过SPAC上市，我们认为一些谨慎可能是有意义的。
因此，现在Shift是公开的，而且是新资本化的。让我们看看当它进入季度报告的最佳状态时，它的股票会发生什么。(显然，如果它摇摇欲坠，对SPAC来说是一个坏标记，但反过来，成功的交易可能会给SPAC-Mageddon带来更多动力。)。
再做几件事，我们就完事了。独角兽出场者本周表现不错。首先，Datto的IPO持续推进。该公司本周设定了初步价格，这可能使其估值超过40亿美元。同样在本周，Roblox宣布已提交上市申请，尽管是私下提交的。它也价值数十亿美元。最后，DoubleVerify希望在明年初上市，筹资高达50亿美元。
并不是所有的流动性都来自公开市场，就像我们看到的本周Twilio收购Segment的交易一样，交易所对这笔交易进行了深入研究，以确定其价格是否合理。
我们自然会跑很长时间，所以这里只有几个快速的东西可以添加到你周末精神上的茶和咖啡阅读中！
这是我们帮助整理的一篇TechCrunch文章，深入挖掘了媒体初创公司的现状。太好玩了！
在这里，你可以跟踪DuckDuckGo在与谷歌和必应竞争时的增长情况。
本周股票交易非常有趣，所以如果你有30分钟的空闲时间，一定要收看。
下周，我们将更深入地挖掘第三季度的风险投资数据，你可以在这里找到关于女性创始人的预感，这是我们周五更深入地讨论的一个话题。
note_en:
It was an active week in the technology world broadly, with big news from Facebook and Twitter and Apple. But past the headline-grabbing noise, there was a steady drumbeat of bullish news for unicorns, or private companies worth $1 billion or more.
  The Exchange spent a good chunk of the week looking into different stories from unicorns, or companies that will soon fit the bill, and it’s surprising to see how much positive financial news there was on tap even past what we got to write about.
  Databricks,   for example,  disclosed a grip of financial data to TechCrunch ahead of regular publication, including the fact that it grew its annual run rate (not ARR) to $350 million by the end of Q3 2020, up from $200 million in Q2 2019. It’s essentially IPO ready, but is not hurrying to the public markets.
 Sticking to our theme,  Calm    wants more money for a huge new valuation, perhaps as high as $2.2 billion  which is not a surprise. That’s more good unicorn news. As was the report that “ India’s Razorpay [became a] unicorn after its new $100 million funding round” that came out this week.
 Razorpay is only one of  a number of Indian startups that have become unicorns during COVID-19. (And here’s  another digest out this week concerning a half-dozen startups that became unicorns “amidst the pandemic.”)
 There was enough good unicorn news lately that we’ve lost track of it all. Things like  Seismic raising $92 million, pushing its valuation up to $1.6 billion from a few weeks ago. How did that get lost in the mix?
 All this matters because while the IPO market has captured much attention in the last quarter or so, the unicorn world has not sat still. Indeed, it feels that unicorn VC activity is the highest we’ve seen since 2019.
 And, as we’ll see in just a moment, the grist for the unicorn mill is getting refilled as we speak. So, expect more of the same until something material breaks our current investing and exit pattern.
  What do unicorns eat? Cash. And many, many VCs raised cash in the last seven days.
 A partial list follows. It could be that investors are looking to lock in new funds before the election and whatever chaos may ensue. So, in no particular order, here’s who is newly flush:
 $450 million  for OpenView, $800 million  for Canaan, $840 million  for True Ventures, $950 million  for Lead Edge Capital
 Something called Benson Capital Partners has put together a $50 million fund. Gayle Benson, for whom the firm is named, owns several New Orleans sports teams,  per Forbes.
 Plus Venture Capital, built by two former 500 Startups Mena investors  according to fundsglobalMENA, has raised $60 million.
 First Round is  looking for $220 million, former Google exec Kai-Fu Lee’s Sinovation Ventures is  looking for a billion, while  Khosla wants a bit more.
 All that capital needs to go to work, which means lots more rounds for many, many startups. The Exchange also caught up with a somewhat new firm this week: Race Capital. Helmed by Alfred Chuang, formerly or BEA who is an angel investor now in charge of his own fund, the firm has $50 million to invest.
 Sticking to private investments into startups for the moment, quite a lot happened this week that we need to know more about. Like API-powered Argyle raising $20 million from Bain Capital Ventures  for what FinLedger calls “unlocking and democratizing access to employment records.” TechCrunch is currently  tracking the progress of  API-led startups.
 On the fintech side of things, M1 Finance  raised $45 million for its consumer fintech platform in a Series C, while another roboadvisor,  Wealthsimple, raised $87 million, becoming a unicorn at the same time. And while we’re in the fintech bucket, Stripe  dropped $200 million this week for Nigerian startup Paystack. We need to pay more attention to the African startup scene. On the smaller end of fintech,  Alpaca raised $10 million more to help other companies become Robinhood.
 A few other notes before we change tack. Kahoot  raised $215 million due to a boom in remote education, another trend that is inescapable in 2020 as part of the larger edtech boom ( our own Natasha Mascarenhas has more).
 Turning from the private market to the public, we have to touch on SPACs for just a moment. The Exchange got on the phone this week with Toby Russell from Shift, which is now a public company, trading after it merged with a SPAC, namely Insurance Acquisition Corp. Early trading is  only going so well, but the CEO outlined for us precisely why he pursued a SPAC, which was actually interesting:
 Shift could have gone public via an IPO, Russell said, but prioritized a SPAC-led debut because his firm wanted to optimize for a capital raise to keep the company growing.
 How so? The private investment in public equity (PIPE) that the SPAC option  came with ensured that Shift would have hundreds of millions in cash.
 Shift also wanted to minimize what the CEO described as market risk. A SPAC deal could happen regardless of what the broader markets were up to. And as the company made the choice to debut via a SPAC in April, some caution, we reckon, may have made some sense.
 So now Shift is public and newly capitalized. Let’s see what happens to its shares as it gets into the groove of reporting quarterly. (Obviously, if it flounders, it’s a bad mark for SPACs, but, conversely, successful trading could lead to a bit more momentum to SPAC-mageddon.)
 A few more things and we’re done. Unicorn exits had a good week. First, Datto’s IPO continues to move forward. It  set an initial price this week, which could value it above $4 billion. Also this week, Roblox  announced that it has filed to go public, albeit privately. It’s worth billions as well. And finally, DoubleVerify is  looking to go public for as much as $5 billion early next year.
 Not all liquidity comes via the public markets, as we saw this week’s  Twilio purchase of Segment, a deal  that The Exchange dug into to find out if it was well-priced or not.
  We’re running long naturally, so here are just a few quick things to add to your weekend mental tea-and-coffee reading!
 Here’s a TechCrunch piece we helped put together that  digs into the current state of media startups. It’s fun!
  Here’s where  you can track the growth of DuckDuckGo as it takes on Google and Bing.
 Equity was a bundle of fun this week,  so make sure to tune in if you have 30 minutes of free time.
 Next week we are digging more deeply into Q3 venture capital data, a foretaste of which you can find  here, regarding female founders, a topic that  we returned to Friday in more depth.
title: 风投在选举前重新加载，独角兽强势领先
title_en: VCs reload ahead of the election as unicorns power ahead
